text: The surgeon performs a robotic cholecystectomy, initially separating the drain tract. The gallbladder wall is pulled down near the duodenum, revealing the common duct. The surgeon uses scissors to dissect, removing fat from the inferior edge of the gallbladder.

The surgeon switches back to using the hook instrument to dissect along the lateral edge of the gallbladder, identifying a posterior artery near the bipolar device. The surgeon then moves to the medial aspect, encountering an anterior branch of the artery and a veil of fat covering the anterior gallbladder wall. Dissection continues to expose the gallbladder cystic duct, progressing along the medial edge and over the artery.

The surgeon creates a window behind the gallbladder, establishing a critical view for further dissection. The gallbladder is opened from the top down, revealing the anterior and posterior arteries. The surgeon prepares to dissect behind these arteries to access the common channel.

The surgeon uses scissors to dissect beneath the anterior and posterior arteries, creating a larger window behind them. There is some bleeding observed, but it is confined to the gallbladder area. The surgeon thins the adventitial fibrovascular fatty tissue around the cystic duct to facilitate clip placement.

The surgeon enlarges the window around the anterior and posterior branches of the artery to place three clips. The cystic duct is thinned out to create a suitable landing zone for clips. Scissors are used for precise dissection due to their pointed nature, allowing access to tight areas.
The surgeon secures clips onto the cystic duct, which is now exposed with minimal surrounding fat, completing the operation.
